quote: Jack Ketch stands there, elbows locked, for all the world like a carpenter
subject: Jack Ketch
note: Jack Ketch was a famous English executioner, known for his brutality and incompetence. A brief explanation of his notoriety would add context.

quote: diversions of a London hanging: no catcalls, jugglers, or pickpockets.
subject: London hanging
note: Public executions in London were major public events, often attracting large crowds and various forms of entertainment. A short description of the atmosphere would be helpful.

quote: squadron of lobsterbacks drills and marches round the base
subject: lobsterbacks
note: "Lobsterbacks" was a common nickname for British soldiers, referring to their red coats. A brief explanation of the term would be useful.

quote: On Sir Isaac Newton’s temperature scale, where freezing is zero
subject: Isaac Newton
note: Newton proposed a temperature scale based on 0 for freezing and 12 for body temperature. This is different from Fahrenheit or Celsius, and a brief explanation of its context and limitations would be helpful.

quote: If Herr Fahrenheit were here with one of his new quicksilver-filled
subject: Fahrenheit
note: Daniel Gabriel Fahrenheit was a physicist and instrument maker best known for inventing the alcohol thermometer (1709) and the mercury thermometer (1714), and for developing a temperature scale now named after him. This is a very early reference to his work.

quote: books in Hebrew, Greek, and Latin pocked with the occult symbols of Alchemists and Kabalists
subject: alchemy
note: Alchemy was a philosophical and protoscientific tradition practiced throughout Europe, Africa and Asia. A brief explanation of its goals and methods would be helpful.

quote: Navy’s very largest men-of-war is able to moor at its end.
subject: men-of-war
note: A "man-of-war" was a type of warship, typically a large sailing vessel with multiple gun decks. A brief explanation of its role and significance in naval warfare would be useful.

quote: Cartesian number-line—what Leibniz would call the Ordinate
subject: Gottfried Wilhelm von Leibniz
note: Leibniz was a prominent German polymath and philosopher who developed calculus independently of Newton. The reference to the "Ordinate" alludes to his work in mathematics and coordinate systems.

quote: Slavers, merchants of rum and molasses and tea and tobacco
subject: Slave trade
note: The reference to slavers and merchants dealing in rum, molasses, tea, and tobacco highlights the interconnectedness of the slave trade with other commodities in the colonial economy. A brief explanation of the triangular trade would be helpful.

quote: Back in America and among Puritans
subject: Puritans
note: The Puritans were a significant religious group in colonial America. Providing context on their beliefs, social structure, and influence in New England would be beneficial.

quote: Christendom
subject: Christendom
note: The term 'Christendom' refers to the historical and cultural concept of a unified Christian world. Explaining its significance in the 17th/18th century context, especially in contrast to other cultural spheres like Islam, would be useful.

quote: A Peace has been signed at Utrecht. France gets Spain.
subject: Treaty of Utrecht
note: The Treaty of Utrecht was a series of peace treaties that ended the War of the Spanish Succession. A summary of the treaty's main provisions and its impact on European power dynamics would be helpful.

quote: the slave trade.
subject: Asiento
note: The Asiento was a contract granted by the Spanish crown to various European powers for the right to provide African slaves to the Spanish colonies. Explaining its significance and the powers involved would be useful.

quote: The War of the Spanish Succession,” says Ben, “whose cause was the death in Madrid of King Carlos the Sufferer.
subject: War of the Spanish Succession
note: The War of the Spanish Succession was a major European conflict triggered by the death of Charles II of Spain. Providing context on the competing claims to the Spanish throne and the alliances involved would be helpful.

quote: The Glorious Revolution!
subject: Glorious Revolution
note: The Glorious Revolution was the overthrow of King James II of England. Explaining the causes, key figures, and consequences of this event would be beneficial.

quote: William and Mary!
subject: William and Mary
note: William III and Mary II were the joint monarchs who replaced James II after the Glorious Revolution. Providing context on their reign and their significance in English history would be helpful.

quote: The Civil War,” says Ben.
subject: English Civil War
note: The English Civil War was a series of conflicts between Parliamentarians and Royalists. Explaining the causes, key figures (like Cromwell), and consequences of this event would be beneficial.

quote: later came the Restoration
subject: Restoration
note: The Restoration refers to the return of the monarchy in England with Charles II after the period of the Commonwealth. Explaining the political and social changes that occurred during this period would be helpful.

quote: Archbishop Laud
subject: Archbishop Laud
note: William Laud was the Archbishop of Canterbury under Charles I. Explaining his role in the religious tensions that led to the English Civil War would be helpful.

quote: Charles the First. The Puritans paid him back by chopping off the head of that same Charles in Charing Cross, in the year of our lord sixteen hundred and forty-nine.
subject: Charles I
note: Charles I was the King of England whose execution marked a turning point in English history. Providing context on his reign, his conflicts with Parliament, and his eventual execution would be helpful.

quote: Cromwell
subject: Oliver Cromwell
note: Oliver Cromwell was a key figure in the English Civil War and the subsequent Commonwealth. Explaining his role as Lord Protector and his impact on English society would be helpful.

quote: tallow-chandlers’ sons do not go to grammar school
subject: Grammar school
note: Grammar schools in this period were secondary schools that focused on classical education, primarily for the upper classes. Explaining the curriculum and social implications would be helpful.

quote: He watched the head of King Charles the First being chopped off.
subject: Charles I
note: Charles I was King of England, Scotland, and Ireland until his execution in 1649 during the English Civil War. Understanding the circumstances of his execution provides context for the political climate of the time.

quote: Archbishop Laud had caused his ears and his nose to be cut off in Star Chamber
subject: Star Chamber
note: The Star Chamber was an English court of law that became notorious for its secret and arbitrary proceedings, often used by the monarchy to suppress dissent. Knowing this helps understand the father's persecution.

quote: Against all Kings.
subject: Kings
note: The father's agitation 'against all Kings' indicates a republican or anti-monarchist sentiment, which was a significant political position during and after the English Civil War.

quote: Shocking how different this place is from London.
subject: London
note: Understanding the social and political differences between Boston and London in the 17th century is important. Boston was a Puritan settlement, while London was the center of the English monarchy and Restoration culture.

quote: the coins that are being stamped out by Sir Isaac Newton in the Royal Mint at the Tower of London.
subject: Isaac Newton
note: Isaac Newton was Master of the Royal Mint at this time. This is a detail that highlights his involvement in practical matters beyond his scientific pursuits.

quote: the coins that are being stamped out by Sir Isaac Newton in the Royal Mint at the Tower of London.
subject: Royal Mint
note: The Royal Mint is the institution responsible for producing the coinage of England (and later, Great Britain). Knowing this helps understand the context of Newton's role.

quote: the coins that are being stamped out by Sir Isaac Newton in the Royal Mint at the Tower of London.
subject: Tower of London
note: The Tower of London served as the location of the Royal Mint at this time. It was also a prison, armory, and treasury.

quote: Spanish—the same coins that are changing hands, at this moment, in Lima, Manila, Macao, Goa, Bandar Abbas, Mocha, Cairo, Smyrna, Malta, Madrid, the Canary Islands, Marseilles.
subject: Spanish coins
note: The prevalence of Spanish coinage in global trade reflects Spain's economic power and colonial reach at the time. Understanding this context is important for grasping the global economy of the era.

quote: Piece of Eight, or a bit, as they are called.
subject: Piece of Eight
note: The 'Piece of Eight' was a Spanish silver coin widely used in international trade. Knowing its value and use helps understand the economic transactions in the novel.

quote: Caribbean to go a-pirating on the Spanish Main.
subject: Spanish Main
note: The Spanish Main refers to the mainland territories of the Spanish Empire in the Americas, particularly the Caribbean coast. It was a prime target for pirates and privateers.

quote: Sir Isaac could probably work out in his head.
subject: Isaac Newton
note: Reference to Newton's mathematical abilities, specifically his work with conic sections, highlights his genius and its relevance to the scientific understanding of the world at the time.

quote: The Hypothesis of Vortices is pressed with many difficulties.
subject: Hypothesis of Vortices
note: The Hypothesis of Vortices was a theory of gravity proposed by René Descartes, which Newton refuted with his law of universal gravitation. This reference highlights the ongoing scientific debates of the era.

quote: arms of the King of Spain are branded into his left shoulder, and so he is probably Angolan.
subject: Angolan
note: Angola was a major source of slaves for the transatlantic slave trade. The branding indicates the slave's origin and ownership, highlighting the brutal realities of the slave trade.

quote: abducted by Africans fiercer than he, chained up in a hole in Luanda
subject: Luanda
note: Luanda was a major port in Angola and a key hub for the transatlantic slave trade. Understanding its role in the slave trade provides context for the slave's experience.

quote: his brethren in London (and that is a very good assumption)
subject: religious conflict in England
note: This refers to the ongoing tensions between various Protestant sects (like the fictional 'Barkers') and the established Church of England, a major theme in the book's historical context.

quote: Lobsterbacks tromping after them in leapfrogging blocks
subject: British soldiers
note: 'Lobsterbacks' was a common nickname for British soldiers, referring to their red coats.

quote: that fell Sect during their halcyon days of Cathedral-sacking and King-killing
subject: English Civil War
note: This alludes to the role of radical Puritan factions during the English Civil War, including the execution of Charles I and the destruction of religious art and architecture.

quote: Some would say it’s the unnatural philosophers that are to blame for Protestants fighting Protestants in England and Catholics everywhere else.
subject: Natural Philosophy
note: This reflects the period's anxieties about the social and religious impact of new scientific ideas associated with natural philosophy.

quote: As when your own Judges finally went up to Salem and pointed out that the people there were going crazy.
subject: Salem Witch Trials
note: This refers to the Salem Witch Trials, a period of mass hysteria and persecution in colonial Massachusetts.

quote: Sir Isaac—who is the President of the Royal Society
subject: Isaac Newton
note: Isaac Newton was a towering figure in the scientific revolution and later became President of the Royal Society.

quote: the King’s Arrow—going to build ships for the Navy.
subject: King's Arrow
note: The King's Arrow was a mark placed on trees reserved for use by the Royal Navy, indicating they were the property of the Crown.

quote: Harvard College is.
subject: Harvard College
note: Harvard College, founded in 1636, was one of the earliest institutions of higher learning in the American colonies.

quote: Some of them have a dour Puritan look about them
subject: Puritans
note: Puritans were a significant religious group in colonial New England, known for their strict moral code and emphasis on religious piety.

quote: They guess that he has come on some Alchemical errand
subject: alchemy
note: Alchemy was a proto-scientific tradition that sought to transform base metals into gold and discover the elixir of life.

quote: vis viva is conducted into Dr. Waterhouse’s gear-boxes
subject: vis viva
note: Vis viva is Latin for 'living force.' In early formulations of mechanics, it was a concept related to kinetic energy. Understanding this term helps to grasp the scientific context of Waterhouse's work.

quote: He’s not entirely satisfied with Harvard College then!?
subject: Harvard College
note: Harvard College (now Harvard University) was founded in 1636 and was a prominent institution in the Massachusetts Bay Colony. Knowing its role and status provides context for Waterhouse's alternative institute.

quote: He is a Fellow of the Royal Society!
subject: Royal Society
note: The Royal Society was (and is) a prestigious scientific institution in England. Being a Fellow signifies high standing in the scientific community. This establishes Enoch Root's credibility.

quote: fallen quite under the spell of Herr Leibniz
subject: Gottfried Wilhelm von Leibniz
note: Gottfried Wilhelm von Leibniz was a prominent German polymath and philosopher. His association with Waterhouse and the negative reaction of the Harvard dons is important to understand.

quote: him that stole the calculus from Sir Isaac
subject: calculus
note: This refers to the calculus priority dispute between Leibniz and Newton. Understanding this controversy adds depth to the characters' opinions and scientific rivalries.

quote: notwithstanding Sir Isaac’s having exploded the old ways
subject: Isaac Newton
note: Sir Isaac Newton was a hugely influential physicist and mathematician. Knowing his impact on science helps understand the context of 'exploding the old ways'.

quote: Mill—designed after Leibniz’s principles—that he imagines will discover new truths through computation!
subject: computing
note: This refers to early mechanical computing devices. Understanding Leibniz's ideas about computation and its potential for discovering new truths is crucial.

quote: Their colleagues are muttering to them words like “Hanover” and “Ansbach.”
subject: Hanover
note: The mention of Hanover and Ansbach alludes to the Hanoverian succession in England. Knowing that the Electress Sophia of Hanover was in line to succeed Queen Anne, and that Caroline of Ansbach was her granddaughter-in-law, provides context for the dons' reaction to the letter.

quote: Queen Anne were already dead and buried, and the Hanovers on the throne.
subject: Queen Anne
note: Queen Anne was the reigning monarch of England at this time. The dons' behavior suggests they are anticipating her death and the succession of the House of Hanover.

quote: the peace of Westphalia did.
subject: Peace of Westphalia
note: The Peace of Westphalia (1648) ended the Thirty Years' War in the Holy Roman Empire and the Eighty Years' War between Spain and the Dutch Republic. It is often seen as a turning point in European history, establishing the modern state system.

quote: the removal of the King’s head from the rest of the King
subject: Charles I
note: This refers to the execution of King Charles I of England in 1649, a pivotal event in the English Civil War.

quote: Cromwell smashed the Royalists for the umpteenth and final time at Worcester.
subject: Oliver Cromwell
note: Oliver Cromwell was a key figure in the English Civil War and later Lord Protector of England, Scotland, and Ireland. The Battle of Worcester in 1651 effectively ended Royalist hopes of restoring the monarchy at that time.

quote: Charles the Second ran off to Paris
subject: Charles II
note: Charles II fled to France after the Battle of Worcester. He lived in exile until the Restoration of the monarchy in 1660.

quote: Through the Dutch Republic, of course.
subject: Dutch Republic
note: The Dutch Republic (c. 1581-1795) was a major European power during this period, known for its trade, naval strength, and relative religious tolerance.

quote: look in on a Mr. Huygens in the Hague.
subject: Christiaan Huygens
note: Christiaan Huygens (1629-1695) was a prominent Dutch physicist, mathematician, astronomer and inventor. He made significant contributions to optics and mechanics, and improved the design of pendulum clocks.

quote: the Navigation Act. And a sea-war against the Dutch.
subject: Navigation Acts
note: The Navigation Acts were a series of English laws restricting the use of foreign ships for trade between England and its colonies. These acts were designed to benefit English shipping and trade, and were a major cause of conflict with the Dutch Republic, leading to several Anglo-Dutch Wars.

quote: Rich Tories, no less!
subject: Tories
note: The term 'Tory' originally referred to those who supported the hereditary right of the monarch, as opposed to the Whigs, who favored parliamentary supremacy. The terms emerged in English politics during the Exclusion Crisis (1678-1681).

quote: before Natural Philosophy as we know it.
subject: Natural Philosophy
note: Natural Philosophy was the term used to describe the study of nature in the period before the rise of modern science. It encompassed what we now call physics, chemistry, biology, and other sciences.

quote: Francis Bacon, Galileo, Descartes
subject: Francis Bacon
note: Francis Bacon (1561-1626) was an English philosopher, statesman, scientist, jurist, and author. He served both as Attorney General and as Lord Chancellor of England. Bacon is considered one of the fathers of modern science, advocating for empirical, inductive reasoning known as the scientific method.

quote: Francis Bacon, Galileo, Descartes
subject: Galileo Galilei
note: Galileo Galilei (1564-1642) was an Italian astronomer, physicist and engineer, sometimes described as a polymath. Galileo has been called the "father of observational astronomy", the "father of modern physics", the "father of the scientific method", and the "father of modern science". His work was controversial due to his support of heliocentrism.

quote: Francis Bacon, Galileo, Descartes
subject: René Descartes
note: René Descartes (1596-1650) was a French philosopher, mathematician, and scientist. Dubbed the father of modern western philosophy, much of subsequent Western philosophy is a response to his writings, which are studied closely to this day. He is best known for the philosophical statement "I think, therefore I am".

quote: Alchemy.
subject: alchemy
note: Alchemy was a philosophical and protoscientific tradition practiced throughout Europe, Africa, and Asia. It aimed to purify, mature, and perfect certain objects. Common goals were the transmutation of base metals into noble metals (gold); creating an elixir of immortality; and creating panaceas able to cure any disease.

quote: Monsieur LeFebure, who was the apothecary to the French Court.
subject: Nicolas Le Febvre
note: Nicolas Le Febvre (c. 1610-1669) was a French chemist and apothecary. He served as the apothecary to Louis XIV and was known for his work in alchemy and chemistry.

quote: Louis the Fourteenth. His formal coronation was in 1654.
subject: Louis XIV
note: Louis XIV (1638-1715), known as the Sun King, was King of France from 1643 until his death. His reign of 72 years and 110 days is the longest of any monarch of a sovereign country in European history. He was a major patron of the arts and sciences, and his court at Versailles became a model for other European monarchs.

quote: pendulums were an old idea—but he did something simple and beautiful
subject: pendulum clock
note: The pendulum clock was a significant invention in timekeeping. While the principle of the pendulum was known earlier, Huygens's improvements made it a reliable and accurate timekeeping device.

quote: New Model Army.
subject: New Model Army
note: The New Model Army was formed in 1645 by the Parliamentarians during the English Civil War. It was distinguished by its professional soldiers, religious discipline, and merit-based promotions, and was instrumental in defeating the Royalists.

quote: John Wilkins
subject: John Wilkins
note: John Wilkins (1614-1672) was an English clergyman, natural philosopher and author, and one of the founders of the Royal Society.

quote: Cromwell and his New Model Army
subject: New Model Army
note: The New Model Army was a key force in the English Civil War, led by Oliver Cromwell. Understanding its role helps contextualize the political climate.

quote: Oxford, meaning only to pay a call on John Wilkins
subject: John Wilkins
note: John Wilkins was a prominent figure in the scientific revolution, a founder of the Royal Society, and known for his interest in cryptography and mechanical inventions. Knowing this gives context to his possession of the Cryptonomicon.

quote: Warden of Wadham College, which is part of the University of Oxford
subject: Wadham College
note: Wadham College, Oxford was a center of scientific and philosophical inquiry in the 17th century, particularly associated with John Wilkins and the early Royal Society. Knowing this highlights the intellectual environment of the time.

quote: Natural Philosophy
subject: Natural Philosophy
note: Natural Philosophy is the historical term for what we now call science. Understanding this helps interpret the characters' motivations and activities.

quote: he married Cromwell’s sister
subject: Oliver Cromwell
note: Oliver Cromwell was Lord Protector of England. Knowing this gives context to the social implications of Wilkins marrying his sister.

quote: Experimental Philosophical Clubb met. Christopher Wren, Robert Boyle, Robert Hooke
subject: Robert Boyle
note: Robert Boyle was a pioneering chemist and physicist, known for Boyle's Law. His presence in the Experimental Philosophical Clubb highlights its scientific importance.

quote: Experimental Philosophical Clubb met. Christopher Wren, Robert Boyle, Robert Hooke
subject: Robert Hooke
note: Robert Hooke was a polymath scientist and inventor, known for his work on microscopy and elasticity. His presence in the Experimental Philosophical Clubb highlights its scientific importance.

quote: Huygens’s house in the Hague
subject: Christiaan Huygens
note: Christiaan Huygens was a prominent Dutch physicist, mathematician, and astronomer, known for his work on optics, mechanics, and timekeeping. His invention of the pendulum clock is a key plot point.

quote: Galileo had timed his experiments
subject: Galileo Galilei
note: Galileo Galilei was a pivotal figure in the scientific revolution, known for his work on astronomy, physics, and mathematics. The contrast between his methods and Huygens's highlights the progress in scientific measurement.
